title: Listening - Part 4: Living in a Tiny House
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
topic: five people discussing their decision to move into a custom-built tiny house

content.audio_script: Speaker 1: I’m a digital nomad, and [21]I moved into a tiny house on wheels because I wanted the freedom to move my home whenever I got bored of the scenery[/21]. I wanted a life of total mobility. [26]The most unexpected benefit has been the massive reduction in my environmental footprint[/26]. I’m now using solar power and composting, and it feels great to be living in a way that is so much more sustainable. It’s made me much more conscious of my water and energy usage in everything I do. || Speaker 2: I’d been struggling with debt for years, so [22]I built a tiny house primarily because I wanted to live mortgage-free and take control of my finances[/22]. It was a survival strategy. However, [27]the biggest challenge has been the lack of privacy when I have guests over[/27]. You can't really have a private conversation when your living room is also your bedroom and kitchen. I’ve had to become much more selective about who I invite into my space, which has been a bit of a social adjustment. || Speaker 3: I’m an artist, and [23]I moved to a tiny house in the woods because I wanted to eliminate all the distractions of city life and focus entirely on my work[/23]. I wanted to simplify everything. [28]What I found most difficult was the physical work involved in maintaining the house[/28], especially during the winter. Chopping wood and dealing with frozen pipes is a full-time job. I definitely underestimated how much manual labor it would take to keep a tiny house running smoothly in a rural setting. || Speaker 4: I recently retired and [24]I chose a tiny house because I wanted to downsize and get rid of decades of accumulated 'stuff' that was weighing me down[/24]. I wanted a mental reset. [29]The most rewarding aspect has been the incredible sense of peace and stillness I feel in such a small, curated space[/29]. There is no clutter, and therefore no mental noise. It’s become my sanctuary, and I find I’m much more creative and calm than I ever was in my five-bedroom family home. || Speaker 5: I’d always lived in tiny city apartments, so [25]I built a tiny house because I wanted a home that was actually designed for my specific needs rather than a generic developer's layout[/25]. I wanted custom functionality. [30]The most significant outcome has been the discovery of a vibrant and supportive 'tiny house' community online[/30]. We share tips on everything from storage solutions to legal parking spots. It’s given me a whole new social circle of people who share my values of minimalism and independence.

task1.title: Task One
task1.instruction: For questions 21–25, choose from the list (A–H) what primarily motivated each speaker to move to a tiny house.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 wanted the freedom to move his home and have total mobility.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 wanted to live mortgage-free and take control of her finances.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 wanted to eliminate city distractions and simplify her life for her art.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 wanted to downsize and get rid of accumulated possessions for a mental reset.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 wanted a home designed specifically for her needs rather than a generic layout.

options.B: to follow a trend among their peers
options.C: to have the ability to travel frequently
options.D: to be closer to their place of work
options.F: to rid themselves of excessive belongings
options.G: to find a more tranquil environment for their career
options.H: to have a property tailored to their requirements

task2.title: Task Two
task2.instruction: For questions 26–30, choose from the list (A–H) what each speaker mentions about their experience.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 mentions a massive reduction in his environmental footprint.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 mentions the lack of privacy when having guests over.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 found the physical maintenance, like chopping wood, to be the most difficult part.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 highlights the sense of peace and stillness in the small, curated space.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 highlights the social circle and community she found online.

options.A: a realization that the space was too small
options.B: a positive change in their ecological impact
options.C: the difficulty of getting planning permission
options.D: an improvement in their mental tranquility
options.E: the pleasure of saving on maintenance costs
options.F: the effort required for domestic upkeep
options.G: the challenge of hosting social gatherings
options.H: the value of the connections they made
